seoTitle: How to Categorize Refunds in QuickBooks
metaDescription: Learn how to categorize customer and vendor refunds in QuickBooks so your income and expense reports stay accurate instead of overstated.
intro: Refunds can come from a customer return or a vendor reimbursement, and each needs to be categorized correctly to avoid overstating your income or expenses. This guide explains how to record and categorize refunds properly in QuickBooks.

steps.heading: Identify the type of refund
steps.body: Determine whether the refund is money going back to a customer or money coming back from a vendor.

steps.heading: Choose the correct refund transaction
steps.body: Use the refund receipt option for customer refunds or the vendor credit option for money coming back from a supplier.

steps.heading: Assign the correct category
steps.body: Match the refund to the same account or category as the original transaction so it offsets correctly.

steps.heading: Review your reports
steps.body: Check your income and expense reports to confirm the refund reduced the right numbers.

faq.question: Should a refund reduce income or count as an expense?
faq.answer: A customer refund typically reduces income, while a vendor refund usually reduces an expense, rather than creating a new separate category.

faq.question: What if the refund is only partial?
faq.answer: You can enter a partial refund amount, and it will offset the original transaction by that amount only.

faq.question: Does a refund affect sales tax?
faq.answer: Yes, if the original sale included tax, the refund should typically adjust the tax liability as well.

faq.question: Can I track refunds separately for reporting purposes?
faq.answer: Yes, some businesses set up a dedicated refunds account to keep an eye on return volume separately from regular sales.
